title: Goals & Scope of Financial Management
simple_html:
Financial management is about deciding how a company raises money, where it invests that money, and how much it returns to owners. Done well, it keeps the firm solvent today and more valuable tomorrow.

The core idea

Every business answers three money questions: What should we invest in? (the investment, or capital budgeting, decision), How should we pay for it? (the financing decision), and What do we do with the profits? (the dividend decision). Financial management is the discipline of answering these three so the firm is worth more.

Why it matters

The agreed primary goal is to maximize shareholder wealth — that is, the long-run value of the owners' stake, usually measured by the share price. This is preferred over simply "maximizing profit," because profit ignores timing, risk, and the cash actually generated. A project can boost this year's profit yet destroy value if it is too risky or pays off too slowly.

Key takeaways

  • Three decisions: investment, financing, and dividend — these define the whole field.
  • Goal: maximize shareholder (owner) wealth, not just accounting profit.
  • Cash and timing matter: a dollar today is worth more than a dollar later, and risky dollars are worth less.
  • Agency problem: managers may pursue their own interests, so owners use incentives and oversight to keep them aligned.
  • Stakeholders count: sustainable value also depends on customers, employees, lenders, and society — not shareholders alone.

In short, financial management gives a firm a clear scorecard (value) and a clear set of levers (the three decisions) to move it.
intermediate_html:
Financial management is the planning, organizing, directing, and controlling of a firm's financial resources. Its scope is captured by three interdependent decisions, all judged against one objective: maximizing the wealth of the firm's owners.

The three financial decisions

  • Investment (capital budgeting) decision: Which long-term assets and projects should the firm commit capital to? This determines the size and risk of the asset base. (See Lesson 4, Capital Budgeting.)
  • Financing decision: What mix of debt and equity should fund those assets? This shapes the capital structure and the cost of capital. (See Lessons 6 and 7.)
  • Dividend decision: How much profit is returned to shareholders versus retained for reinvestment? (See Lesson 8.)

Profit maximization vs. wealth maximization

An older view held that firms should maximize profit. Modern finance rejects this as the operating goal for three reasons:

IssueProfit maximizationWealth maximization
TimingIgnores when cash arrivesDiscounts cash to present value
RiskIgnores uncertaintyPenalizes risk via the discount rate
MeasureAccounting profit (manipulable)Market value / share price

Wealth maximization means maximizing the present value of expected future cash flows to owners — operationally, the share price for a listed firm.

The agency problem

In modern corporations, ownership (shareholders) is separated from control (managers). Managers may pursue perks, empire-building, or excessive caution rather than owner value. These agency costs are managed through performance-linked pay, equity ownership, board oversight, debt covenants, and the market for corporate control (takeovers).

Worked mini-case

A manager can choose Project A (adds 50,000 to this year's reported profit but is highly risky and pays off in year 5) or Project B (adds 30,000 of profit, low risk, cash within a year). A profit-maximizing rule picks A. A wealth-maximizing rule discounts both for time and risk; once Project A's distant, risky cash flows are discounted heavily, Project B may produce the higher present value — and the share price reflects B's superior risk-adjusted contribution. The lesson: the goal you choose changes the decision you make.

How to apply it

  1. Frame any financial question as one (or more) of the three decisions.
  2. Evaluate options by their effect on owner wealth — discounted, risk-adjusted cash flows — not on next quarter's profit.
  3. Check incentives: are the people deciding rewarded for value creation?

Finance also serves stakeholders (lenders, employees, customers, regulators). The modern reconciliation is enlightened value maximization: long-run owner value cannot be sustained while abusing stakeholders, so stakeholder welfare is a constraint and an input, not a competing objective.
full_html:
Financial management concerns the acquisition, allocation, and management of a firm's financial resources to achieve its objectives. As an academic field it crystallized in the mid-twentieth century, shifting from a descriptive, institutional focus (how securities are issued) to an analytical, decision-oriented science grounded in valuation. This lesson defines the goal of the firm and maps the scope of financial decision-making that the rest of the course elaborates.

Scope: the three financial decisions

The function reduces to three interrelated decisions (Van Horne & Wachowicz, 2008):

  • The investment / capital budgeting decision allocates capital to long-term assets and projects, defining the firm's risk-return profile. It is developed in Lesson 4 (NPV, IRR, PI) and depends on the discounting logic of Lesson 2.
  • The financing decision selects the proportion of debt and equity — the capital structure (Lesson 7) — and thereby the firm's cost of capital (Lesson 6).
  • The dividend / distribution decision determines the split between retained earnings and payouts (Lesson 8).

The decisions are not separable in practice: financing affects the cost of capital used to evaluate investments, and dividend policy interacts with financing because retained earnings are a source of equity (the residual link explored in Lesson 9, Working Capital, for short-term mirror images of these choices).

The objective function: shareholder wealth maximization

Finance theory adopts maximization of shareholder wealth — the present value of expected future cash flows accruing to owners, proxied by share price — as the firm's normative objective. The case against the older profit maximization criterion is threefold (Solomon, 1963): it is ambiguous (which profit, over what horizon?), it ignores the timing of returns, and it ignores risk. Wealth maximization remedies all three because share price embeds discounting for both time (Lesson 2) and risk (Lesson 5, the CAPM and required returns).

The intellectual foundation is the separation of investment and consumption decisions under perfect capital markets (Fisher, 1930): regardless of an owner's consumption preferences, the firm maximizes every owner's utility by accepting all projects with positive net present value. This Fisher Separation Theorem is why "maximize the share price" is a defensible single objective rather than a crude simplification — it makes the manager's problem independent of any individual shareholder's tastes.

The agency problem and corporate governance

In the modern public corporation, ownership is dispersed and separated from managerial control (Berle & Means, 1932). Jensen and Meckling (1976) modeled the resulting agency relationship: managers (agents) may divert value through perquisites, empire-building, excessive risk-aversion (to protect their human capital), or short-termism. The associated agency costs comprise monitoring costs, bonding costs, and residual loss.

Mechanisms that mitigate agency costs include: (1) incentive alignment via equity-based compensation and options; (2) board monitoring and independent directors; (3) the disciplining effect of debt, which commits free cash flow to interest payments and reduces managerial slack (Jensen, 1986, free cash flow hypothesis — directly relevant to capital structure in Lesson 7); (4) the market for corporate control, where underperforming managers face takeover; and (5) external monitoring by auditors, analysts, and regulators. Note that some mechanisms create their own conflicts — e.g., shareholder-debtholder conflicts (asset substitution, underinvestment) addressed by covenants.

Shareholders versus stakeholders

A long-running debate contrasts shareholder primacy (Friedman, 1970 — the social responsibility of business is to increase its profits within the rules of the game) with stakeholder theory (Freeman, 1984), which holds the firm accountable to employees, customers, suppliers, communities, and the environment. Jensen (2001) proposed enlightened value maximization as a reconciliation: a firm cannot maximize long-run value while systematically destroying stakeholder relationships, so stakeholder welfare enters as a constraint and a value driver rather than a rival objective. Contemporary ESG and the 2019 Business Roundtable restatement of corporate purpose reflect this tension; the rigorous finance position is that genuine, durable value creation and responsible stakeholder treatment are complements over a long horizon.

Worked illustration

Suppose two mutually exclusive projects each cost 100,000. Project A returns a lump sum of 150,000 in year 5; Project B returns 35,000 per year for five years. Profit maximization (total nominal gain) ranks them 50,000 vs. 75,000, favoring B — but for the wrong reasons. At a 10% required return, NPV(A) = 150,000/1.10^5 - 100,000 = 93,138 - 100,000 = -6,862, while NPV(B) = 35,000 x 3.7908 - 100,000 = 132,678 - 100,000 = +32,678. Wealth maximization selects B because it correctly discounts the timing of cash flows. Had A been less risky and delivered sooner, the ranking could flip — the point is that the objective and the discount rate, not raw profit, drive the value-maximizing choice.

Common mistakes and misconceptions

  • Equating profit with cash: accounting profit includes non-cash items and accruals; value depends on cash flow and its timing.
  • Treating wealth maximization as short-term price manipulation: the objective is the intrinsic long-run value, not a managed quarterly number.
  • Ignoring risk: two projects with equal expected returns are not equally valuable; the riskier one is worth less.
  • Assuming managers automatically serve owners: agency conflict is the default, not the exception, and must be designed against.
  • Posing shareholders vs. stakeholders as zero-sum: over a long horizon they are largely complementary.

Cross-links

This lesson is the organizing frame for the course: the investment decision is operationalized in Lesson 4 using the time value tools of Lesson 2 and the risk-adjusted discount rates of Lessons 5 and 6; the financing and dividend decisions appear in Lessons 7 and 8; and Lesson 3 (ratio analysis) provides the diagnostic lens for whether these decisions are creating value. Working capital (Lesson 9) applies the same investment-financing logic to short-term assets and liabilities.
